title: Behavioral Economics: Loss Aversion, the Decoy Effect, and the Power of Free
description: Forty questions on the most famous findings in behavioral economics — why losses hurt about twice as much as equivalent gains, how a useless third option (the decoy) can flip your choice, and why the word *free* makes us behave irrationally. Each question pairs a tempting wrong answer with an explanation that teaches the underlying mechanism, followed by a reference to the original research or a primary source for verification and further reading. Covers prospect theory, the endowment effect, framing, anchoring, mental accounting, nudges, the ultimatum game, and the Nobel-winning work of Kahneman, Tversky, Simon, and Thaler.
author: Quizbun

questions.title: What does *loss aversion* describe?

options.text: Losses feel psychologically more powerful than gains of the same size
People avoid every financial risk, large or small
People would rather lose a little than win a lot
People stop noticing losses once they become small

questions.explanation: Loss aversion is the finding that *losses loom larger than gains*: the pain of losing $100 is felt more intensely than the pleasure of gaining $100. It was introduced by Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky as part of prospect theory. Note that it is not the same as avoiding all risk — it is specifically about the **asymmetry** between losses and gains measured from a reference point.
questions.references: [Prospect Theory: An Analysis of Decision under Risk](https://www.jstor.org/stable/1914185)

questions.title: Roughly how much more does a loss tend to loom than an equivalent gain, by Tversky and Kahneman's 1992 estimate?

options.text: About twice as large (a coefficient of roughly 2)
About ten times as large
About the same — the asymmetry disappears once measured
About one-tenth as large

questions.explanation: In their 1992 paper *Advances in Prospect Theory*, Tversky and Kahneman estimated the loss-aversion coefficient λ at about **2.25**, meaning losses are weighted roughly 2–2.5 times as heavily as equal gains. This 2:1 rule of thumb is widely cited, though later studies find the exact value varies a lot by person and context.
questions.references: [Advances in Prospect Theory: Cumulative Representation of Uncertainty](https://doi.org/10.1007/BF00122574)

questions.title: Who introduced *prospect theory*, the framework that formalized loss aversion?

options.text: Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky
Richard Thaler and Cass Sunstein
Herbert Simon
Maurice Allais

questions.explanation: Prospect theory was published by psychologists Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky in the journal *Econometrica* in 1979. It described how people actually evaluate risky choices — through gains and losses relative to a reference point — and became the foundation of behavioral economics. Thaler and Sunstein later wrote *Nudge*; Simon and Allais made earlier, separate contributions.
questions.references: [Prospect Theory: An Analysis of Decision under Risk](https://www.jstor.org/stable/1914185)

questions.title: In which year did Kahneman and Tversky publish their landmark *Prospect Theory* paper in *Econometrica*?
questions.explanation: *Prospect Theory: An Analysis of Decision under Risk* appeared in **1979**. It is one of the most cited papers in all of economics and is the work most directly recognized by Kahneman's 2002 Nobel Memorial Prize. (Their refined *cumulative* prospect theory came later, in 1992.)
questions.references: [Prospect Theory: An Analysis of Decision under Risk](https://www.jstor.org/stable/1914185)

questions.title: Which statements about the prospect theory *value function* are correct?

options.text: It measures outcomes as gains and losses from a reference point, not as levels of total wealth
It is steeper for losses than for equivalent gains
It is concave for gains but convex for losses (diminishing sensitivity)
It is defined over a person's total final wealth, as in expected utility theory
Gains and losses of the same size are given equal psychological weight

questions.explanation: Prospect theory's value function has three signature features: it is **reference-dependent** (outcomes are coded as gains or losses from a reference point, not as final wealth — that wealth-based view belongs to classical expected utility theory); it is **steeper for losses** (loss aversion); and it shows **diminishing sensitivity**, bending concave in the gain domain and convex in the loss domain. The last two distractors describe the older, rational model that prospect theory was designed to replace.
questions.references: [Prospect Theory: An Analysis of Decision under Risk](https://www.jstor.org/stable/1914185)

questions.title: How does *loss aversion* differ from ordinary *risk aversion*?

options.text: Loss aversion is about the gain/loss asymmetry around a reference point; risk aversion is a general dislike of uncertainty
They are two names for exactly the same thing
Loss aversion applies only to gambling; risk aversion only to insurance
Risk aversion means preferring losses, loss aversion means preferring gains

questions.explanation: Risk aversion is the classical idea that people prefer a sure thing to a gamble of equal expected value. Loss aversion is sharper and more specific: outcomes are judged as **gains or losses relative to a reference point**, and the loss side simply weighs more. This is why a loss-averse person can be risk-*seeking* when all options are losses — a behavior plain risk aversion cannot explain.
questions.references: [Prospect Theory: An Analysis of Decision under Risk](https://www.jstor.org/stable/1914185)

questions.title: Prospect theory predicts people are risk-averse for gains but risk-seeking for losses. What is this pattern called?

options.text: The reflection effect
The certainty effect
The endowment effect
The halo effect

questions.explanation: The **reflection effect** is the mirror-image flip in risk attitude across the reference point. Offered a sure gain of $500 versus a coin flip for $1,000, most people take the sure $500 (risk-averse). But facing a sure *loss* of $500 versus a coin flip to lose $1,000 or nothing, most gamble (risk-seeking) — they will take a chance to avoid a certain loss. This is a direct consequence of the value function's convex shape in the loss domain.
questions.references: [Prospect Theory: An Analysis of Decision under Risk](https://www.jstor.org/stable/1914185)

questions.title: In the classic *Asian disease* problem, why do people flip from a cautious choice to a risky one?

options.text: The identical outcomes are framed once as 'lives saved' (a gain) and once as 'deaths' (a loss)
The numbers are secretly changed between the two versions
People choose at random when probabilities are involved
The second version offers a genuinely better deal

questions.explanation: Tversky and Kahneman's 1981 problem describes a disease expected to kill 600 people. When programs are framed as how many will be *saved* (a gain frame), most people pick the certain option; when the *same* programs are framed as how many will *die* (a loss frame), most switch to the gamble. The outcomes are mathematically identical — only the framing changed — which is why this is the textbook demonstration of the **framing effect** and the reflection effect working together.
questions.references: [The Framing of Decisions and the Psychology of Choice](https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.7455683)

questions.title: What is the *endowment effect*?

options.text: People value a thing more highly merely because they own it
People value gifts less than items they bought
Rich people value money less than poor people do
People donate more when they feel wealthy

questions.explanation: The endowment effect is the tendency to demand much more to give up an object than you would have been willing to pay to acquire it in the first place. Ownership shifts your reference point, so parting with the item now registers as a **loss** — which is why the endowment effect is usually explained as a consequence of loss aversion.
questions.references: [Experimental Tests of the Endowment Effect and the Coase Theorem](https://www.journals.uchicago.edu/doi/10.1086/261737)

questions.title: In the Cornell coffee-mug experiment (Kahneman, Knetsch & Thaler, 1990), what did the researchers find?

options.text: Mug owners demanded about twice as much to sell as buyers were willing to pay
Buyers and sellers quickly agreed on a single fair price
Owners gave their mugs away for free almost immediately
Buyers offered more than sellers were asking

questions.explanation: Students randomly given a university mug (worth about $6) demanded roughly **$5–7** to sell it, while students who had not been given one were willing to pay only about **$2–3** to buy the same mug. Because owners and non-owners were assigned at random, the gap could not reflect different tastes — only the act of owning. The experiment also tested the Coase theorem and found trade was much lower than rational models predict.
questions.references: [Experimental Tests of the Endowment Effect and the Coase Theorem](https://www.journals.uchicago.edu/doi/10.1086/261737)

questions.title: What is the most common explanation for the endowment effect?

options.text: Loss aversion — once you own something, giving it up is coded as a loss
People are simply bad at arithmetic
Owners always have secret information about quality
Sellers are greedier than buyers by nature

questions.explanation: Once an object is yours, it becomes part of your reference point, so selling it feels like a loss while buying merely forgoes a gain — and losses weigh more. That asymmetry inflates the selling price relative to the buying price. (Some later research adds other contributing factors, such as ownership triggering attachment, but loss aversion remains the standard account.)
questions.references: [Experimental Tests of the Endowment Effect and the Coase Theorem](https://www.journals.uchicago.edu/doi/10.1086/261737)

questions.title: What is *status quo bias*?

options.text: A preference for keeping things as they are, treating a change as a potential loss
A preference for constant change and novelty
The belief that the present is worse than the past
A tendency to copy whatever the majority does

questions.explanation: Status quo bias is the disproportionate preference for the current state of affairs. Because moving away from the status quo means giving something up, the potential losses of changing loom larger than the potential gains — another close relative of loss aversion. It is why people stick with default insurance plans, subscriptions, and settings even when better options exist.
questions.references: [Status Quo Bias in Decision Making](https://doi.org/10.1007/BF00055564)

questions.title: In investing, the *disposition effect* is the tendency to…

options.text: Sell winning assets too early and hold losing assets too long
Sell losing assets quickly and let winners run
Buy only assets that have recently fallen in price
Trade as little as possible regardless of price moves

questions.explanation: Coined by Hersh Shefrin and Meir Statman in 1985, the disposition effect describes how investors rush to *realize gains* (locking in the pleasure of a win) but cling to *paper losses* to avoid the pain of a realized loss — and to dodge the regret of admitting a bad pick. It follows from loss aversion plus mental accounting, and it tends to hurt returns.
questions.references: [The Disposition to Sell Winners Too Early and Ride Losers Too Long: Theory and Evidence](https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1540-6261.1985.tb05002.x)

questions.title: What is the *sunk cost fallacy*?

options.text: Continuing something because of past, unrecoverable investment rather than future value
Refusing to ever spend money on long-term projects
Always finishing a task faster than planned
Ignoring all past costs when making any decision

questions.explanation: A sunk cost is money, time, or effort already spent that cannot be recovered. The fallacy is letting it drive future decisions — sitting through a bad film because you paid for the ticket, or funding a failing project because of all you have poured in. Rational choice looks only at *future* costs and benefits. It is also called the *Concorde fallacy*, after the supersonic jet whose development continued long past any economic sense.
questions.references: [The Psychology of Sunk Cost](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/0749597885900494)

questions.title: Which of the following are commonly explained as consequences of *loss aversion*?

options.text: The endowment effect
Status quo bias
The disposition effect
The anchoring bias
The availability heuristic

questions.explanation: The endowment effect, status quo bias, and the disposition effect all flow from the same root: once a reference point is set, the prospect of *giving something up* weighs more than an equal-sized gain. Anchoring (leaning on an arbitrary starting number) and the availability heuristic (judging frequency by how easily examples come to mind) are genuine biases too, but they are **judgment heuristics**, not products of loss aversion.
questions.references: [Experimental Tests of the Endowment Effect and the Coase Theorem](https://www.journals.uchicago.edu/doi/10.1086/261737) · [Status Quo Bias in Decision Making](https://doi.org/10.1007/BF00055564) · [The Disposition to Sell Winners Too Early and Ride Losers Too Long: Theory and Evidence](https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1540-6261.1985.tb05002.x)

questions.title: Prospect theory says people *overweight* small probabilities. Which everyday pair of behaviors does this help explain?

options.text: Buying lottery tickets and buying insurance at the same time
Always saving instead of ever spending
Refusing to gamble under any circumstances
Treating a 1% chance exactly like a 0% chance

questions.explanation: Prospect theory replaces objective probabilities with a *weighting function* that overweights rare events. The same person who pays for a tiny chance of a huge jackpot (the lottery) will also pay to remove a tiny chance of a huge loss (insurance) — both involve giving extra psychological weight to low-probability outcomes. A purely rational expected-value calculator would often do neither.
questions.references: [Prospect Theory: An Analysis of Decision under Risk](https://www.jstor.org/stable/1914185)

questions.title: What is the *decoy effect* (also called the attraction or asymmetric-dominance effect)?

options.text: Adding a third, clearly inferior option shifts choice toward one of the two original options
Hiding the most expensive option makes people spend less
Offering many options makes people choose faster
Removing all but one option guarantees a sale

questions.explanation: The decoy effect occurs when introducing a third option — the *decoy* — that is obviously worse than one of the existing options changes which of the original two people prefer. The decoy is never meant to be chosen; it exists to make a *target* option look better by comparison. It is a classic violation of rational choice, where an irrelevant alternative should not sway your pick.
questions.references: [Adding Asymmetrically Dominated Alternatives: Violations of Regularity and the Similarity Hypothesis](https://academic.oup.com/jcr/article/9/1/90/1839380)

questions.title: In Dan Ariely's *Economist* subscription example, what role did the $125 print-only option play?

options.text: A decoy that made the equally priced $125 print+web bundle look like an obvious bargain
The most popular choice among readers
A cheaper alternative that undercut the web-only plan
A premium tier aimed at libraries, not individuals

questions.explanation: *The Economist* listed three options: web-only for $59, print-only for $125, and print+web for $125. Nobody rationally wants print-only when print+web costs the *same* — but its presence makes the bundle look like a steal (you get the web for free!). Ariely found that with the decoy present, 84% chose the $125 bundle and 16% the web-only plan; with the decoy removed, preferences flipped to 32% bundle and 68% web-only. The useless option changed real choices.
questions.references: [Predictably Irrational: The Hidden Forces That Shape Our Decisions](https://books.google.com/books?id=kApgNtTsAccC)

questions.title: In Ariely's *Economist* study, when the print-only decoy was on the menu, what percentage of students chose the $125 print+web bundle? (whole number)
questions.explanation: With all three options shown, **84%** picked the print+web bundle and 16% picked web-only; no one chose print-only. After Ariely removed the never-chosen decoy, the split reversed to 32% bundle and 68% web-only. The decoy alone shifted a majority toward the pricier choice — a striking demonstration that our preferences are constructed by comparison, not fixed.
questions.references: [Predictably Irrational: The Hidden Forces That Shape Our Decisions](https://books.google.com/books?id=kApgNtTsAccC)

questions.title: Why is an effective decoy described as *asymmetrically dominated*?

options.text: It is worse than one option in every respect, but not worse than the other, making the first look superior
It is better than both other options on every attribute
It is identical to one of the other options
It is the cheapest option on every attribute

questions.explanation: A decoy is *dominated* by the target option (the target beats it on every attribute) but *not* dominated by the competitor — that one-sided, or asymmetric, relationship is the trick. Because our minds latch onto easy comparisons, the clearly-better-than-the-decoy target gets an artificial boost, while the competitor (which the decoy does not cleanly lose to) is left looking ambiguous.
questions.references: [Adding Asymmetrically Dominated Alternatives: Violations of Regularity and the Similarity Hypothesis](https://academic.oup.com/jcr/article/9/1/90/1839380)

questions.title: The decoy effect breaks a principle of rational choice. Which one?

options.text: Regularity / independence of irrelevant alternatives — adding an option shouldn't raise another option's share
Transitivity — if A beats B and B beats C, then A beats C
Completeness — every pair of options can be compared
The law of supply and demand

questions.explanation: *Regularity* says that adding an option to a set can never increase the probability of choosing one of the existing options — a close cousin of the *independence of irrelevant alternatives*. The decoy effect violates exactly this: introducing the dominated decoy *raises* the target's share. Huber, Payne, and Puto demonstrated this in 1982, exposing a gap in standard choice models.
questions.references: [Adding Asymmetrically Dominated Alternatives: Violations of Regularity and the Similarity Hypothesis](https://academic.oup.com/jcr/article/9/1/90/1839380)

questions.title: How does the *compromise effect* differ from the decoy effect?

options.text: People gravitate to the middle option of three to avoid extremes — no dominated decoy is required
It only happens when one option dominates all the others
It makes people always choose the cheapest option
It is just another name for the decoy effect

questions.explanation: Both are *context effects* — your choice depends on the surrounding options — but they work differently. In the compromise effect, presenting small/medium/large makes the **middle** option attractive simply because it looks like a safe, balanced choice (a key reason menus and pricing pages so often have three tiers). The decoy effect instead relies on a deliberately inferior, dominated option to steer you toward a specific target.
questions.references: [Adding Asymmetrically Dominated Alternatives: Violations of Regularity and the Similarity Hypothesis](https://academic.oup.com/jcr/article/9/1/90/1839380)

questions.title: Which researchers first documented the attraction (asymmetric-dominance) effect in a 1982 study?

options.text: Huber, Payne, and Puto
Kahneman and Tversky
Shefrin and Statman
Thaler and Sunstein

questions.explanation: Joel Huber, John Payne, and Christopher Puto published *Adding Asymmetrically Dominated Alternatives: Violations of Regularity and the Similarity Hypothesis* in the *Journal of Consumer Research* in 1982. It was the first controlled demonstration that a dominated decoy could lift a competing option's market share, and it launched decades of research on context effects in marketing and psychology.
questions.references: [Adding Asymmetrically Dominated Alternatives: Violations of Regularity and the Similarity Hypothesis](https://academic.oup.com/jcr/article/9/1/90/1839380)

questions.title: What is the *zero price effect*?

options.text: Demand for an item jumps disproportionately when its price falls all the way to free
Free items are always worth exactly nothing to consumers
People ignore any product that costs nothing
Lowering any price by one cent always doubles demand

questions.explanation: Identified by Kristina Shampanier, Nina Mazar, and Dan Ariely in their 2007 paper *Zero as a Special Price: The True Value of Free Products*, the zero price effect is the surge in demand that happens specifically at a price of zero — far larger than the small change from, say, 1 cent to 0 cents would justify. *Free* is treated not as just another low price but as a category of its own.
questions.references: [Zero as a Special Price: The True Value of Free Products](https://web.mit.edu/ariely/www/MIT/Papers/zero.pdf)

questions.title: In the Hershey's Kiss vs. Lindt truffle experiment, what happened when the Kiss dropped from 1¢ to free (and the Lindt from 15¢ to 14¢)?

options.text: Most shoppers switched to the Kiss, even though the price gap between the two stayed the same
Demand for both candies fell sharply
Nothing changed, because the price difference was unchanged
Everyone switched to the more expensive Lindt truffle

questions.explanation: When a Lindt truffle cost 15¢ and a Kiss cost 1¢, about 73% chose the (better) truffle and 27% the Kiss. Then both prices were cut by exactly 1 cent — Lindt to 14¢, Kiss to **free** — leaving the 14-cent gap identical. Yet choices flipped: about 69% now took the free Kiss and only 31% the truffle. Because the relative price was unchanged, ordinary demand theory cannot explain the switch — only the special pull of *free* can.
questions.references: [Zero as a Special Price: The True Value of Free Products](https://web.mit.edu/ariely/www/MIT/Papers/zero.pdf)

questions.title: Ariely argues that *free* makes us irrational largely because it removes which feeling?

options.text: The fear of loss — a free item has no downside, so loss aversion no longer bites
The fear of missing out on a better deal
The desire to appear generous to others
The urge to compare prices across stores

questions.explanation: Ariely's explanation links the power of *free* directly back to loss aversion. Paying even one cent carries a possible downside: if the product disappoints, you have lost that cent and feel you made a bad call. A free item carries **no risk of loss at all** — there is no way to lose — so it feels uniquely safe and irresistibly attractive. Remove the possibility of loss and the brain stops weighing the choice carefully.
questions.references: [Zero as a Special Price: The True Value of Free Products](https://web.mit.edu/ariely/www/MIT/Papers/zero.pdf)

questions.title: Amazon's free-shipping promotion boosted sales everywhere except France for a time. What went wrong?

options.text: France charged a tiny fee (about 20 cents) instead of truly free shipping, which killed the boost
France banned free shipping by law
French customers preferred to pay full shipping
Amazon never offered the promotion in France at all

questions.explanation: Ariely recounts that Amazon's free-shipping-over-a-threshold offer lifted sales in every market but France, where the local team had set shipping to about 1 franc (roughly 20 cents) rather than zero. Sales there did not move until the charge was changed to genuinely **free** — at which point France saw the same jump as everywhere else. A 20-cent difference is economically trivial, but the leap from *something* to *nothing* is psychologically enormous.
questions.references: [Predictably Irrational: The Hidden Forces That Shape Our Decisions](https://books.google.com/books?id=kApgNtTsAccC)

questions.title: A rigged wheel of fortune that stopped on 10 or 65 shifted people's guesses about how many African nations are in the UN. Name the one-word bias this demonstrates.
questions.explanation: This is **anchoring**. In Tversky and Kahneman's 1974 study, participants saw a wheel (secretly rigged to land on 10 or 65), then estimated the percentage of African countries in the UN. Those who saw 10 guessed about 25% on average; those who saw 65 guessed about 45%. A plainly irrelevant number dragged their estimates with it. Anchoring is one of three heuristics introduced in their landmark *Judgment under Uncertainty* paper.
questions.references: [Judgment under Uncertainty: Heuristics and Biases](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17835457/)

questions.title: What is *mental accounting*?

options.text: Treating money differently depending on arbitrary mental categories, as if dollars weren't interchangeable
Keeping a precise written budget of every transaction
The accounting method used by central banks
Calculating compound interest in your head

questions.explanation: Introduced by Richard Thaler, mental accounting describes how people sort money into separate mental 'buckets' — vacation money, grocery money, a tax refund treated as 'free' cash — and spend from each differently, even though money is fungible (a dollar is a dollar). It explains why someone might splurge a windfall while refusing to touch their savings for the same purchase.
questions.references: [Mental Accounting and Consumer Choice](https://pubsonline.informs.org/doi/10.1287/mksc.4.3.199)

questions.title: What does *hyperbolic discounting* (present bias) describe?

options.text: Overvaluing immediate rewards over larger later ones, with preferences that can reverse as time passes
Valuing all future rewards exactly as much as present ones
Discounting the future at a single constant interest rate
Refusing any reward that is not immediate

questions.explanation: Hyperbolic discounting captures our bias toward the present: a reward available *right now* feels far more valuable than the same reward next week, even though we are much more patient about choices far in the future. This produces preference reversals — you plan to start a diet tomorrow, but when tomorrow arrives the immediate temptation wins. It is the engine behind procrastination, under-saving, and many self-control problems.
questions.references: [Golden Eggs and Hyperbolic Discounting](https://doi.org/10.1162/003355397555253)

questions.title: In Thaler and Sunstein's book *Nudge*, what is a 'nudge'?

options.text: A change in how choices are presented that steers behavior without banning options or changing incentives
A law that forces people to make the healthy choice
A tax designed to make bad choices expensive
An advertisement that exaggerates a product's benefits

questions.explanation: A nudge is any aspect of the *choice architecture* that predictably alters behavior while preserving freedom of choice — placing fruit at eye level, or setting a helpful default. Thaler and Sunstein call this *libertarian paternalism*: it gently guides people toward better outcomes without forbidding anything or significantly changing costs. Mandates and taxes are explicitly **not** nudges, because they restrict options or change incentives.
questions.references: [Nudge: The Final Edition](https://yalebooks.yale.edu/book/9780300262285/nudge/)

questions.title: Countries with *opt-out* organ donation have far higher donor rates than *opt-in* countries. Which effect is at work?

options.text: The default effect — people tend to stick with the pre-set option
The decoy effect
The anchoring effect
The zero price effect

questions.explanation: In opt-in systems you are a non-donor unless you act; in opt-out systems you are a donor unless you act. Because changing the default takes effort and feels like a deliberate choice (and inertia plus status quo bias keep people put), the default overwhelmingly wins. Johnson and Goldstein's 2003 *Science* paper *Do Defaults Save Lives?* found consent roughly **doubles** under opt-out — one of the most cited examples of a nudge.
questions.references: [Do Defaults Save Lives?](https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.1091721)

questions.title: In the *ultimatum game*, responders often reject low offers (say, $2 out of $10) and so get nothing. What does this reveal?

options.text: People will pay a price to punish unfairness — fairness can override pure self-interest
People always accept any free money, however small
People cannot understand simple probabilities
Responders never reject any offer in practice

questions.explanation: A purely self-interested responder should accept any positive offer — $2 beats $0. Yet across many studies (starting with Güth, Schmittberger, and Schwarze in 1982), low offers are routinely rejected, leaving both players with nothing. People will sacrifice real money to punish what feels unfair, showing that **fairness and reciprocity** are genuine motives that standard economic models leave out.
questions.references: [An Experimental Analysis of Ultimatum Bargaining](https://doi.org/10.1007/BF01703954)

questions.title: Iyengar and Lepper's famous *jam study* (2000) suggested what about offering more choices?

options.text: A 24-jam display attracted more browsers but produced far fewer purchases than a 6-jam display
More jams always led to more sales
Shoppers ignored both displays equally
Only the cheapest jam ever sold

questions.explanation: At a grocery tasting booth, the large display of 24 jams drew more passers-by to stop (about 60% vs 40% for the 6-jam table), but only about **3%** of those who stopped at the big display bought any jam, compared with roughly **30%** at the small one. The lesson — *choice overload* — is that too many options can paralyze decisions. Note: later replications have been mixed and the effect appears to depend on context, so treat the jam study as a vivid illustration rather than an iron law.
questions.references: [When Choice Is Demotivating: Can One Desire Too Much of a Good Thing?](https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.79.6.995)

questions.title: The *peak–end rule* says our memory of an experience is dominated by…

options.text: Its most intense moment and its ending, largely ignoring how long it lasted
Its total duration above all else
Only the very first moment
A precise average of every moment, second by second

questions.explanation: Kahneman and colleagues found that we judge past experiences mainly by their **peak** (most intense point) and their **end**, while the length barely registers — a phenomenon called *duration neglect*. In Redelmeier and Kahneman's 1996 colonoscopy study, patients whose procedure was extended with extra but milder discomfort at the end actually remembered it as *less* unpleasant. It is why how something finishes can matter more than how long it took.
questions.references: [Patients' Memories of Painful Medical Treatments: Real-Time and Retrospective Evaluations of Two Minimally Invasive Procedures](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/8857625/)

questions.title: Which of these figures have received the Nobel Memorial Prize in Economic Sciences?

options.text: Herbert Simon
Daniel Kahneman
Richard Thaler
Amos Tversky
Dan Ariely

questions.explanation: Herbert Simon won in 1978 (bounded rationality), Daniel Kahneman in 2002 (prospect theory and judgment under uncertainty, shared with Vernon Smith), and Richard Thaler in 2017 (behavioral economics). Amos Tversky — Kahneman's closest collaborator — did **not** win only because he died in 1996, and the prize is not awarded posthumously; Kahneman repeatedly said it was a shared achievement. Dan Ariely, though hugely influential in popularizing the field, has not received the prize.
questions.references: [Herbert A. Simon – Facts](https://www.nobelprize.org/prizes/economic-sciences/1978/simon/facts/) · [Daniel Kahneman – Facts](https://www.nobelprize.org/prizes/economic-sciences/2002/kahneman/facts/) · [Richard H. Thaler – Facts](https://www.nobelprize.org/prizes/economic-sciences/2017/thaler/facts/)

questions.title: Herbert Simon's concept of *bounded rationality* says that people…

options.text: Make reasonable decisions within limits of information, time, and brainpower — 'satisficing' rather than optimizing
Are completely rational and always optimize perfectly
Are entirely irrational and decide at random
Can only make decisions with the help of a computer

questions.explanation: Herbert Simon argued that real decision-makers cannot gather all information or compute the perfect answer, so instead of *maximizing* they *satisfice* — they search until they find an option that is good enough. Bounded rationality reframed humans as reasonable-but-limited rather than flawless calculators, and it laid intellectual groundwork that Kahneman, Tversky, and Thaler later built on.
questions.references: [A Behavioral Model of Rational Choice](https://www.jstor.org/stable/1884852)

questions.title: After watching dramatic coverage of a plane crash, people overestimate the danger of flying. Which mental shortcut is this?

options.text: The availability heuristic
The endowment effect
The sunk cost fallacy
Hyperbolic discounting

questions.explanation: The availability heuristic is judging how likely something is by how *easily examples come to mind*. Vivid, recent, or heavily reported events (plane crashes, shark attacks) spring to mind readily, so we overrate their frequency — even though statistically mundane risks like car travel are far deadlier. It is another of the heuristics Tversky and Kahneman catalogued.
questions.references: [Judgment under Uncertainty: Heuristics and Biases](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17835457/)

questions.title: What is the *IKEA effect*?

options.text: People place a higher value on things they partly built or assembled themselves
People prefer furniture that is cheap above all else
People dislike any product with a foreign name
People buy more when stores are laid out as a maze

questions.explanation: Named after the flat-pack furniture you assemble yourself, the IKEA effect — documented by Norton, Mochon, and Ariely in 2012 — is the boost in value people give to objects they helped create, whether IKEA boxes, origami, or LEGO. Importantly, the effect only appears when the labor **succeeds**: half-built or destroyed creations get no such bump. The effort itself, when it pays off, breeds attachment.
questions.references: [The IKEA Effect: When Labor Leads to Love](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcps.2011.08.002)

questions.title: The *Allais paradox* exposed people's pull toward certainty. What is the *certainty effect*?

options.text: Outcomes that are certain are overweighted relative to ones that are merely very probable
People treat a 99% chance exactly like a 100% chance
People prefer risky gambles to any sure thing
Certainty has no effect on how people choose

questions.explanation: Maurice Allais devised his paradox in 1953 to show that real choices violate expected utility theory's independence axiom. People place a special premium on **guaranteed** outcomes: dropping a probability from 100% to 99% feels far worse than dropping it from 50% to 49%, even though the change is the same. Kahneman and Tversky later folded this *certainty effect* into prospect theory through the probability-weighting function.
questions.references: [Le Comportement de l'Homme Rationnel devant le Risque: Critique des Postulats et Axiomes de l'Ecole Americaine](https://doi.org/10.2307/1907921) · [Prospect Theory: An Analysis of Decision under Risk](https://www.jstor.org/stable/1914185)
